source.title: Monaca Fatal Explosion and Fire - Analysis of Column B

actions_taken: Operator cut gas input flow in response to high rate of temperature change alarm
Operator cut gas further following additional alarm
Plant restarted in January 2011 following $15M investment in rebuilding and safety improvements
Complete Monaca smelter permanently closed in April 2014
event.summary: On 22 July 2010 at 14:20-16:19, a fatal explosion and fire occurred on Column B, a cadmium distillation column at the Horsehead zinc smelter in Monaca, Pennsylvania. The incident resulted from a partial blockage of the column sump that allowed liquid zinc to back-fill up the column, creating a pressure cooker effect. As the column filled with liquid zinc, internal pressure increased, raising the boiling point of zinc from 907°C to 980°C and creating superheated conditions. The column operated outside design parameters with pressure surges as vapour attempted to escape. Eventually, tray walls failed under the increased internal pressure, releasing a surge of superheated zinc vapour and liquid that ignited explosively, destroying the combustion chamber wall and creating a major conflagration in the workplace. The root causes included sump design with restricted clearance (25.625 inches or 65mm), poorly executed column commissioning that allowed formation of sticky zinc oxide dross/liquid zinc emulsion, high and increasing zinc throughput, inadequate sump temperatures during heat-up, and human factors including normalisation of hazardous conditions due to history of column blockages.
recommendations: Increase sump underflow clearance from 1 brick (65mm) to 2 bricks (130-180mm) minimum to allow passage of zinc, dross and debris
Implement best practice standards of management and training
Improve operator awareness and plant information systems
Take all possible steps to prevent air ingress to the column at feed point, condenser and sump
Implement constant checking that column sump is running normally to prevent accumulation of liquid zinc
Avoid too low a run-off operating regime to prevent formation and accumulation of hard zinc compounds
Ensure adequate sump temperatures during column heat-up (target 650°C minimum)
Implement procedures to detect sump blockages early through nostril box inspections for dark trays
Change feed arrangement from top-down to bottom-up needle valve configuration to reduce dross and air entrainment
Maintain steady feed and firing conditions to minimize internal pressure disturbances
Implement monitoring of liquation pot temperatures to prevent excessive iron recycling (target maximum 450°C)
Improve commissioning procedures to prevent formation of sticky zinc oxide dross/liquid zinc emulsion
Establish clear emergency procedures that address sump blockage risks on all column types, not just zinc oxide columns

hazards.name: Molten zinc at elevated temperature and pressure

threats.name: Sump blockage from zinc oxide dross and hard zinc accumulation
Liquid zinc back-fill of column trays
Increased internal column pressure from flooded trays
Tray wall fatigue failure under sustained high pressure
Rapid release of pressurised zinc vapour and superheated liquid
Air ingress to column during feed variations or suction conditions
Excessive iron concentration in recycled zinc

consequences.name: Ignition of zinc vapour and liquid in combustion chamber
Combustion chamber wall failure and explosion
Release of flame and superheated zinc into workplace
Fatality and severe burn injuries to personnel
Damage to plant equipment and building structure
Workplace contamination with zinc oxide ash
Plant shutdown and loss of production

controls.name: Sump design with adequate underflow clearance
controls.barrier_role: Prevent accumulation of dross and debris in sump
controls.lod_basis: Physical design to allow passage of zinc, dross and debris

supporting_text: One important clearance in the sump to avoid blockages is the minimum vertical depth below the underflow and is, at most plants, 130-180 mm ('two bricks' or more). The depth is not only important to allow passage of zinc and associated debris (dross, little bits of brick and mortar)
The dimension is shown as 25 5/8 inches ('1 brick'), about 65 mm. This was far less than most, if not all, zinc refineries.
Many plants increased the dimension to '2 bricks' after the Australian fatality in 1973.
The Noyelles-Godault accidents were both on columns with 70mm - '1 brick' underflows

controls.name: Column commissioning procedure with adequate sump heating
controls.barrier_role: Prevent formation of sticky zinc oxide dross/liquid zinc emulsion during heat-up
controls.lod_basis: Proper heat-up schedule to dry mortar and prevent dross formation

supporting_text: Although the timeline shows the sump burner being lit on 2 July, the Column Heat-Up log shows the sump temperature well below target; this continued right through the warm-up with the highest temperature recorded being 380ºC on 6 July; temperatures well below the target of 650ºC continued for the last few days before feed.
The sump burner is the means to warm the column from the inside and to dry any mortar in the sump or under tray #1.
Witness interviews referred to bricklayers 'working in the sump' and to the sump burner not being present

One of the operators referred specifically to the fact that the sump burner flame (when the burner was present) was not being pulled into the sump, even with the cover tiles off, and thought that there was a problem with the sump.

controls.name: Feed arrangement design to minimize dross and air entrainment
controls.barrier_role: Prevent air ingress and dross formation in column
controls.lod_basis: Bottom-up needle valve configuration to prevent dross and air being drawn down

supporting_text: Below is shown the needle valve and launder system to the feed box of column B. The valve, although at an angle, was 'top down' through the valve, allowing dross and air to be more easily drawn down through the needle valve with the liquid zinc.
The conclusion from the Noyelles-Godault investigation was that the flow should be 'bottom up.'
Although the feed box is designed to settle out dross, some dross and air may still be entrained in the liquid zinc flowing to the column.

controls.name: Steady feed and firing control
controls.barrier_role: Prevent internal pressure disturbances and air ingress
controls.lod_basis: Maintain stable operating conditions to prevent suction and air ingress

supporting_text: The chart shows large variations in the number of ladles fed from one shift to another. Although the needle valve before the feed box will smooth the flow to some extent, it may be that feed variations at Monaca were quite significant. Any variation creates an opportunity for air ingress and the subsequent production of zinc oxide dross.
Steady operation is a reflection of good design and competent and systematic attention to operating detail.
Steady feed and firing minimises such disturbances.

controls.name: Liquation pot temperature control
controls.barrier_role: Prevent excessive iron concentration and hard zinc formation
controls.lod_basis: Maintain liquation pot temperature at maximum 450°C to control iron solubility

supporting_text: However it is of concern that the logged temperatures for liquation pots are frequently considerably higher than the target of not more than 450ºC. For example, column 25 evening shift shows 550ºC and 530ºC for the night of 21 July 2010 and following morning.

If, for example, the liquation pot was being operated at an excessive 550ºC, the run-off recycle would contain 0.3% Fe. The subsequent run-off could then, with the 'new' iron from new strip metal, contain 0.34% Fe. The sump temperature could now easily be low enough to allow clogging with hard metal.

controls.name: Sump blockage detection and monitoring
controls.barrier_role: Detect early signs of sump blockage and back-fill
controls.lod_basis: Regular inspection and monitoring of sump operation and temperature trends

supporting_text: The timeline shows B sump leaking (suggesting blockage) being strapped on 14 July and an air lance being applied to the sump. Leaks occurred again on 15 July. Further strapping took place on 19 July when the sump leaked. Clearly there were continuing problems with the sump.
Sump problems on B column were mentioned in many interviews.
Constant checking that the column sump is running normally, i.e. preventing any possible accumulation of liquid zinc in the column.
Key indications of developing problems in the hours before the accident were not observed and acted upon.

controls.name: Nostril box inspection for dark trays
controls.barrier_role: Detect zinc back-fill in column before catastrophic failure
controls.lod_basis: Visual inspection through peepholes to identify dark trays indicating back-fill

supporting_text: Approximately 10 minutes before the explosion there was an alarm indicating a high rate of temperature change covering the column waste gas temperature, and the operator cut the gas input flow. The action was to 'Item 1 – Waste Gas Temperatures Rapidly Rising.' Had it been to 'Item 2 – Oxide Combustion Unit (Down Draft), Flickering of Vapour,' the procedure would have required the operator to inspect the column through nostril boxes (peepholes next to the column burners) for 'dark trays' – a sign of zinc backfilling the column. As a result no one inspected the nostril boxes.
The events leading up to the previous B column premature shutdown are described, and show that the column clearly backed up. Showing through the nostril boxes were 'black trays' and a 'ring of fire.'

controls.name: Temperature monitoring and alarm system
controls.barrier_role: Detect abnormal temperature rise indicating back-fill and trigger operator response
controls.lod_basis: Automated alarm on waste gas temperature rate of change

supporting_text: Approximately 10 minutes before the explosion there was an alarm indicating a high rate of temperature change covering the column waste gas temperature, and the operator cut the gas input flow. Following a further alarm he cut gas further, and shortly afterwards the column exploded.
The operator actions were exactly those required by the procedure.

controls.name: Emergency shutdown procedure
controls.barrier_role: Reduce gas input to column in response to temperature alarm
controls.lod_basis: Operator procedure to cut gas flow when temperature alarm triggered

supporting_text: The operator actions were exactly those required by the procedure. In my professional opinion it was almost certainly too late to do anything anyway.

controls.name: Combustion chamber explosion relief panels
controls.barrier_role: Relieve pressure from combustion chamber during explosion
controls.lod_basis: Designed to blow out at low over-pressure to prevent structural failure

supporting_text: The combustion chamber wall will explode due to the increased pressure, the explosion panels not taking much over-pressure to blow them out.
This photograph shows the combustion chamber wall of column B, and the site of the explosion, the pressure relief panels being blown out into the workplace.

controls.name: Sump strapping (manual clearing) procedure
controls.barrier_role: Manually clear sump blockages before back-fill occurs
controls.lod_basis: Operator procedure to use rod/strap to clear underflow slot

supporting_text: The timeline shows B sump leaking (suggesting blockage) being strapped on 14 July and an air lance being applied to the sump. Leaks occurred again on 15 July. Further strapping took place on 19 July when the sump leaked.

It is worth noting the distance of the underflow to the external point on the sump of some 150 inches (3.8 m). This is what must be traversed by a rod or strap worked remotely by an operator, or as described by an operator 'No one has X-ray vision.' It is therefore a skilled task to find and clear the underflow slot.

controls.name: Hazard awareness and normalisation prevention
controls.barrier_role: Maintain awareness of hazards and prevent normalisation of dangerous conditions
controls.lod_basis: Safety culture and training to ensure operators recognise and respond to hazard indicators

supporting_text: Human factors played a dominant role. The process is not easy to instrument and awareness of what is happening is vital. Key indications of developing problems in the hours before the accident were not observed and acted upon. In short, because of a history of column blockages and explosions at the Monaca facility, hazardous conditions had been 'normalised.'
Prior to the explosion, many workers reported that everything appeared to be normal.

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
